name: Caves of Nialó & Lladre - Circular through the Mountains of Pradas
name_original: Cuevas de Nialó & Lladre - Circular por los Montes de Pradas
region_intro: Jogging routes around Montblanc traverse a diverse alpine landscape, characterized by dramatic mountain ranges, high alpine meadows, and serene valleys. The region offers a variety of terrains, from challenging ascents over mountain passes to gentler riverside trails and forested sections. Runners can experience routes that provide views of glaciers and alpine lakes, alongside paths winding through dense woodlands. This varied topography makes Montblanc a destination for different running preferences. Best jogging routes around Montblanc The most popular jogging route is Caves of Nialó & Lladre - Circular through the Mountains of Pradas, a 9.7 miles (15.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 19 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores mountainous terrain, offering significant elevation changes. Another top favourite among local runners is Sant Joan de Muntanya – Vall Waterfall loop from Montblanc, a difficult 16.2 miles (26.0 km) path. This route features substantial elevation gain, winding through valleys and past natural features. Local runners also love the Sant Joan de Muntanya – Cave of the Thief loop from Montblanc, a 10.3 miles (16.6 km) trail leading through mountainous landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 32 minutes. Jogging around Montblanc is defined by alpine landscapes, high alpine meadows, and forested valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Montblanc are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 7 reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore Montblanc's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in the Montblanc region?
A: There are over 45 running routes around Montblanc, offering a wide range of experiences. These include paths through dramatic alpine landscapes, high alpine meadows, and serene valleys, catering to various preferences and skill levels.

Q: Are there running routes for all skill levels around Montblanc?
A: Yes, the Montblanc region offers a diverse network of running paths suitable for various abilities. You can find 2 easy routes, 19 moderate routes, and 28 difficult routes, ensuring options for beginners to experienced trail runners.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails in Montblanc?
A: Running trails in Montblanc feature a varied topography. You can expect challenging ascents over mountain passes like Col de Voza or Col de la Seigne, gentle riverside trails, and forested sections. Many routes offer breathtaking views of glaciers such as the Mer de Glace, high alpine meadows, and pristine alpine lakes like Lac Blanc.

Q: Can I find running routes that feature waterfalls or caves in Montblanc?
A: Yes, the region offers routes that pass by natural features like waterfalls and caves. For instance, the challenging route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20147591" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Joan de Muntanya – Vall Waterfall loop from Montblanc&lt;/a&gt; features a waterfall. You can also explore routes near caves such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950310039/caves-of-nialo-lladre-circular-through-the-mountains-of-pradas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caves of Nialó & Lladre - Circular through the Mountains of Pradas&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24543694" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Joan de Muntanya – Cave of the Thief loop from Montblanc&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural landmarks or viewpoints can I see while jogging in Montblanc?
A: While jogging in Montblanc, you can encounter numerous natural landmarks and viewpoints. The region is famous for its glaciers like the Mer de Glace, accessible via the Montenvers rack railway, and stunning alpine lakes such as Lac Blanc. You'll also cross dramatic mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/345714" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de Capafonts–Prades&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic vistas of the surrounding Mont Blanc massif and Aiguilles Rouges Nature Reserve.

Q: Are there any historical sites or interesting places to explore near the running trails?
A: Yes, beyond the natural beauty, you can find interesting sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/433933" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of La Mussara&lt;/a&gt; offer a glimpse into the region's past. The town of Chamonix itself is a vibrant hub with amenities and a rich history as a center for outdoor activities.

Q: What is the best season for running in the Montblanc area?
A: The best season for running in Montblanc largely depends on the elevation and type of route. High alpine trails, especially those traversing mountain passes, are typically best from late spring through early autumn (June to September) when snow has melted. Lower elevation trails and forested sections can be enjoyed earlier in spring and later into autumn, offering beautiful scenery as the seasons change.

Q: Are there family-friendly running routes in Montblanc?
A: While many routes in Montblanc involve significant elevation, there are easier options suitable for families. Look for routes categorized as 'easy' or 'moderate' that stick to lower valleys or well-maintained paths. These often provide a gentler experience without the extreme challenges of high alpine trail running.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails around Montblanc?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on many trails in the Montblanc region, especially on public footpaths. However, specific rules may apply in nature reserves like the Aiguilles Rouges or around certain protected areas. It's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash and check local regulations for any specific route you plan to run to ensure a pleasant experience for everyone.

Q: What do other runners enjoy the most about jogging in Montblanc?
A: The running routes in Montblanc are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from 7 reviews. Runners often praise the dramatic alpine scenery, the variety of terrain from challenging mountain passes to serene valleys, and the well-maintained paths that allow for an immersive outdoor experience.

Q: Are there any circular running routes available in Montblanc?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Montblanc are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20147323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Deport Spring – Cova del Lladre loop from Montblanc&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38285771" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cova del Lladre – Pujada Coma loop from Montblanc&lt;/a&gt;. These loops are ideal for exploring the diverse landscapes without needing to arrange transportation back to your starting point.

Q: Are there amenities like mountain huts or cafes along the running routes?
A: Especially on longer trails or sections of the Tour du Mont Blanc, you'll find numerous mountain huts (refuges) like Rifugio Bertone or Refuge Elena. These offer places for rest, food, and even overnight stays, providing a unique cultural experience. In towns like Chamonix, you'll find a wide range of cafes, pubs, and accommodation options.
tips:
The hermitage of Sant Joan de la Muntanya is one of the most picturesque places in the region and offers views, from its viewpoint, of the Conca de Barberà and the Serra Carbonària and, on days of good visibility, Montserrat and the Pyrenees.

In the hermitage, there is a bell at the entrance and to the right is the chapel of Sant Joan Baptista, excavated in the rock. A fireplace and a kitchen have also been rebuilt to shelter the mountaineers. If you go up to the top of the building, you will reach a small square with some commemorative pieces from various Montblanc organizations and, on the right, you will find the Mirador de Sant Joan, with a book of signatures and the Catalan flag, which is changed only for Christmas and Saint John.

The viewpoint is a red saldón rock, which at the same time acts as the wall and roof of the hermitage. At the viewpoint there is a tile altarpiece that indicates which towns and mountain ranges can be seen: Montblanc, Barberà de la Conca, Forès, Solivella, Serra del Tallat, Miramar, Montserrat, Andorra, El Canigó, the Massif de la Maladeta with Aneto, etc. If you follow the ridge next to the viewpoint, you will reach the Cova de Nialó.

After the Spanish Civil War ended, a group of young people fixed the rooms and covered the roof. But the absence of hermits and the location in the middle of the forest caused the hermitage to deteriorate to the point that, in the early 1990s, it was completely in ruins. It was then that Joan Serafí Serra i Ollé and the brothers Joan and Josep Jàvega i Bulló created the Estrams group (genesis of the Hermits of Sant Joan de la Muntanya),
In 1993, the Estrams group was the catalyst for the desire to recover the hermitage and the Ermitans de Sant Joan de la Muntanya group was formed. This group, made up of around thirty members, went up to the hermitage once a week to have breakfast together and work on different tasks, inside and outside the hermitage. His task meant fixing it almost completely, rebuilding the fountain, placing a copy of the Damian Forment altarpiece, etc. After almost 70 years, the hermitage celebrated the Eucharist again.
Nice detour on the approach to the via ferrata.

The forest where the cave is is beautiful.
Free access cave without the need to be equipped.
After a small detour from the main route, you will find this small waterfall that seems like an oasis of tranquility and freshness. Ideal to rest before continuing with the adventures.
